quantum_threat_notes.shor: Shor's algorithm breaks integer factorization and discrete logarithm problems — renders RSA, DSA, ECC, DH broken on a sufficiently powerful quantum computer
quantum_threat_notes.grover: Grover's algorithm provides quadratic speedup for brute-force search — effectively halves symmetric key/hash security strength

asymmetric_encryption.name: RSA
asymmetric_encryption.quantum_status: BROKEN — all key sizes vulnerable to Shor's algorithm
asymmetric_encryption.recommendation: For encryption/key exchange: migrate to ML-KEM (FIPS 203). For signatures (RSA-PSS, PKCS#1): migrate to ML-DSA (FIPS 204) or SLH-DSA (FIPS 205).

key_exchange.name: ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman)
key_exchange.quantum_status: BROKEN — Shor's algorithm applies to elliptic curve discrete log

key_exchange.name: ML-KEM
key_exchange.quantum_status: SAFE — This is the FIPS 203 standardized name for CRYSTALS-Kyber
key_exchange.recommendation: Preferred PQC KEM — use ML-KEM-768 or ML-KEM-1024 for high security

key_exchange.name: FrodoKEM
key_exchange.quantum_status: SAFE — based on plain Learning with Errors (LWE), no ring/module structure. Most conservative lattice assumption.
key_exchange.recommendation: Very conservative choice — larger keys/ciphertexts than Kyber, but security relies on fewer structural assumptions. Good for very high-assurance contexts.
key_exchange.nist_status: NIST Round 3 alternate candidate — not selected as primary standard, but still actively maintained by Microsoft Research

key_exchange.name: Classic McEliece
key_exchange.classical_security: strong — 50+ years without meaningful attack on core problem
key_exchange.quantum_status: SAFE — code-based (binary Goppa codes). Oldest and most battle-tested PQC assumption. Very large public keys (hundreds of KB to MB range).
key_exchange.recommendation: Extremely conservative choice for long-term data protection. Impractical for most interactive protocols due to large key sizes. Good for store-now-decrypt-later threat model.

key_exchange.name: SABER
key_exchange.quantum_status: SAFE — lattice-based (Module Learning with Rounding / MLWR). No longer actively developed since Kyber was selected.
key_exchange.recommendation: Do not start new deployments — Kyber/ML-KEM is preferred. May be encountered in existing implementations.

key_exchange.name: NewHope
key_exchange.quantum_status: SAFE — ring-LWE based. Historically important (used by Google in TLS experiments 2016), but superseded by Kyber.
key_exchange.recommendation: Do not use in new deployments — superseded by ML-KEM. May be found in legacy/experimental systems.
key_exchange.nist_status: withdrew from NIST process after Round 2

key_exchange.name: NTRU
key_exchange.nist_status: NIST Round 3 finalist, not selected as primary standard

key_exchange.name: BIKE
key_exchange.recommendation: Use with caution — still under NIST evaluation (Round 4)

key_exchange.name: HQC
key_exchange.quantum_status: SAFE — code-based (quasi-cyclic moderate-density parity-check codes). Selected by NIST for standardization in March 2025 as additional KEM alongside ML-KEM.
key_exchange.recommendation: Emerging standard — good as backup/diversity KEM alongside ML-KEM. Standardization in progress.
key_exchange.nist_status: Selected for standardization March 2025 — standard in progress (backup KEM to FIPS 203 / ML-KEM)

digital_signatures.name: CRYSTALS-Dilithium (ML-DSA)
digital_signatures.quantum_status: SAFE — NIST standard FIPS 204 (2024). Lattice-based (Module-LWE + Module-SIS).

digital_signatures.name: ML-DSA
digital_signatures.quantum_status: SAFE — This is the FIPS 204 standardized name for CRYSTALS-Dilithium
digital_signatures.recommendation: Preferred PQC signature scheme — ML-DSA-65 recommended for most use cases

digital_signatures.name: FALCON (FN-DSA)
digital_signatures.quantum_status: SAFE — lattice-based (NTRU lattices). Compact signatures, more complex implementation than Dilithium.
digital_signatures.recommendation: Good for bandwidth-constrained environments. More implementation complexity than ML-DSA.

digital_signatures.name: SPHINCS+ (SLH-DSA)
digital_signatures.quantum_status: SAFE — hash-based stateless signature. Security relies only on hash function properties — most conservative assumption of all PQC signature schemes.
digital_signatures.recommendation: Most conservative choice — no lattice or code assumptions. Larger signatures than ML-DSA/FALCON. Prefer '-s' (small) variants unless performance-critical.

digital_signatures.name: XMSS
digital_signatures.quantum_status: SAFE — hash-based stateful signature. Security relies only on hash function security. IMPORTANT: Stateful — key state MUST be managed carefully; reusing a one-time key pair is catastrophic.
digital_signatures.recommendation: Suitable for firmware signing, certificate authorities, and other low-frequency signing scenarios. Never use if state management cannot be guaranteed. Use SPHINCS+ (SLH-DSA) if stateless operation is required.
digital_signatures.nist_status: NIST SP 800-208 (2020) — approved for use. RFC 8391.

digital_signatures.name: LMS / HSS
digital_signatures.quantum_status: SAFE — hash-based stateful signature. Like XMSS, security depends only on hash functions. IMPORTANT: Stateful — same key-state warning as XMSS applies.
digital_signatures.recommendation: Good for firmware and code signing with limited signature volume. HSS (Hierarchical Signature System) extends LMS for larger key trees. Favored by some US government applications.
digital_signatures.nist_status: NIST SP 800-208 (2020) — approved for use. RFC 8554.

digital_signatures.name: Rainbow
digital_signatures.threat: classical cryptanalysis (2022 attack by Ward Beullens)
digital_signatures.classical_security: BROKEN — completely broken by classical attack in 2022
digital_signatures.quantum_status: BROKEN — eliminated from NIST process due to classical attack
digital_signatures.recommendation: Do not use. Was a NIST Round 3 finalist but broken in 2022.

digital_signatures.name: Picnic
digital_signatures.quantum_status: SAFE — zero-knowledge proof based signature. Security relies on symmetric primitives (LowMC block cipher) only.
digital_signatures.recommendation: Not recommended for production — very large signatures (10s of KB), slow. Mainly of academic interest. Did not advance in NIST process.

symmetric_encryption.name: AES
symmetric_encryption.quantum_status: PARTIAL — Grover halves effective key length. AES-128 → ~64-bit effective (BROKEN). AES-256 → ~128-bit effective (SAFE).
symmetric_encryption.recommendation: Use AES-256. Avoid AES-128 in long-lived or high-security contexts.

symmetric_encryption.name: 3DES (Triple DES)
symmetric_encryption.recommendation: Do not use. Migrate to AES-256.

symmetric_encryption.name: ChaCha20
symmetric_encryption.recommendation: Safe to use, especially with Poly1305 for AEAD

hash_functions.name: MD5
hash_functions.recommendation: Do not use. Migrate to SHA-256 or SHA-3.

hash_functions.name: SHA-1
hash_functions.recommendation: Do not use. Migrate to SHA-256 or SHA-3.

hash_functions.name: SHA-2
hash_functions.quantum_status: PARTIAL — Grover provides quadratic speedup. SHA-256 → ~128-bit preimage security (acceptable). SHA-384/512 → fully safe.
hash_functions.recommendation: SHA-256 acceptable, SHA-384 or SHA-512 preferred for post-quantum.

hash_functions.name: SHA-3
hash_functions.quantum_status: SAFE at 384/512 — different construction than SHA-2 (Keccak sponge)
hash_functions.recommendation: SHA3-256 acceptable, SHA3-384 or SHA3-512 preferred.

hash_functions.name: BLAKE2
hash_functions.quantum_status: BLAKE2b (512-bit output) SAFE. BLAKE2s (256-bit output) marginal.

hash_functions.name: BLAKE3
hash_functions.quantum_status: MARGINAL — 256-bit output yields ~128-bit post-quantum security. Acceptable for most use cases.
hash_functions.recommendation: Acceptable for most contexts. For very high security, use SHA3-512 or BLAKE2b.

mac_and_aead.name: HMAC
mac_and_aead.quantum_status: SAFE if used with SHA-256 or stronger. HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA1 not recommended.

key_derivation.name: PBKDF2
key_derivation.quantum_status: MARGINAL — safe if using SHA-256+ and high iteration counts (≥600k per NIST 2023). Grover may reduce effective strength.
key_derivation.recommendation: Use with HMAC-SHA256+, high iterations. Consider Argon2 for new designs.

key_derivation.name: bcrypt
key_derivation.quantum_status: MARGINAL — bcrypt produces a 184-bit output (hash). Grover reduces effective preimage security to ~92 bits. Additionally, bcrypt has a 72-byte password input limit. Sufficient for most current threat models but not future-proof.
key_derivation.recommendation: Acceptable for passwords. Argon2id preferred for new designs.

key_derivation.name: Argon2
key_derivation.quantum_status: SAFE — memory-hard, Grover threat mitigated by memory hardness
key_derivation.recommendation: Preferred KDF for passwords. Use Argon2id.

protocols_and_suites.name: TLS
protocols_and_suites.quantum_status: NOT SAFE without PQC extensions — key exchange (ECDH) is vulnerable. TLS 1.3 + X25519Kyber768 hybrid is being standardized.
protocols_and_suites.recommendation: Use TLS 1.3 with hybrid PQC key exchange (X25519Kyber768 / FIPS 203).
protocols_and_suites.nist_status: TLS 1.0/1.1 deprecated (RFC 8996); PQC TLS under IETF standardization

protocols_and_suites.name: SSH
protocols_and_suites.quantum_status: NOT SAFE — ECDH/RSA key exchange vulnerable. OpenSSH 9.0+ supports sntrup761x25519 hybrid.
protocols_and_suites.recommendation: Use OpenSSH 9.0+ with post-quantum hybrid KEX.
protocols_and_suites.nist_status: IETF draft for PQC SSH in progress
